problem_statement.Title: :inherited-members option only accepts a single class name; cannot ignore multiple base classes
problem_statement.Description: The autodoc :inherited-members: option behaves as a single-class selector instead of accepting multiple class names. As a result, you cannot list multiple base classes (for example, list,set,tuple) to be ignored when deciding which inherited members to include in autoclass/automodule output.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) inherited_members_option assumes a single value and returns a raw string/sentinel. Location: sphinx/ext/autodoc/__init__.py:inherited_members_option. Current behavior: if arg in (None, True): return 'object' else: return arg. This encodes the single-name assumption and leaves options.inherited_members as a string (or whatever raw value was passed) instead of a canonical collection.

2) filter_members uses equality against the option, assuming a single-name string. Location: sphinx/ext/autodoc/__init__.py:Documenter.filter_members (inner is_filtered_inherited_member). Current check: if cls.__name__ == self.options.inherited_members and cls != self.object: ... . This equality test only matches when the option is a single string and fails for multiple names.

3) The filter relies on ObjectMember.class_ produced by get_class_members. Location: sphinx/ext/autodoc/importer.py:get_class_members. get_class_members sets ObjectMember.class_ to the defining class; filter_members depends on that metadata to decide whether a member is inherited from a given class. Any change to how inherited names are represented must preserve that interaction.

4) Other call sites only test truthiness of options.inherited_members, so converting the option to a collection would preserve behavior there. Locations: sphinx/ext/autodoc/__init__.py (Documenter.get_object_members, ClassDocumenter.get_object_members) and sphinx/ext/autodoc/directive.py (option registration). A repository search found no other cls.__name__ == options.inherited_members comparisons.

5) Back-compat / sentinel handling is encoded as a single string today. Location: sphinx/ext/autodoc/__init__.py:inherited_members_option. The sentinel 'object' (used for flag/no-arg cases) is currently returned as the string 'object'; preserving compatibility requires treating that sentinel as an element of whatever canonical collection is chosen (e.g., {'object'}).

6) End-to-end parsing shapes were not verified by the investigation. Location of potential variance: sphinx/ext/autodoc/directive.py and the directive parsing pipeline. It is unknown whether directive parsing ever supplies a list/tuple (instead of a single string) to inherited_members_option; tests for comma-separated strings, sequence inputs (list/tuple), and flag/no-arg are missing to validate all input shapes.
StepsToReproduce:
Create a module mymod.py containing classes that inherit from built-in containers and other bases, for example:
  - class A(list): pass
  - class B(A): pass
  - class C(list, CustomBase): pass

Create a Sphinx document that uses automodule:: mymod with the option :inherited-members: list,set,tuple (a comma-separated list) or with :inherited-members: ['list', 'tuple'] if passing a sequence is supported.
Build the docs (autodoc enabled) and inspect the generated class member lists for A, B, and C.
Alternatively, use autoclass:: mymod.C with :inherited-members: list and observe members inherited from list.
Observe whether members inherited from the listed base classes (e.g., list, set, tuple) are excluded from the generated output.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: When :inherited-members: names multiple classes (comma-separated string or provided as a list/tuple), autodoc should treat those names as a collection and ignore inherited members defined on any of the listed base classes for the documented classes. The previous flag/no-argument behavior (implicit 'object' sentinel) should continue to work.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Specifying multiple class names in :inherited-members: does not exclude members from all listed bases. The option is treated as a single value, so comma-separated names are not interpreted as multiple class names; members inherited from the intended bases (for example list, set, tuple) remain documented.

For example, there is a class that inherits multiple base classes:
```
class MyClass(Parent1, Parent2, Parent3, ...):
    pass
```
and

```
.. autoclass:: example.MyClass
   :inherited-members: Parent2
```

How should the new `:inherited-members:` work? Do you mean that the member of Parent2 are ignored and the Parent1's and Parent3's are documented? And how about the methods of the super classes of `Parent1`?

problem_statement.Title: autodoc_typehints = "description" produces duplicated *args/**kwargs entries in autodoc output
problem_statement.Description: When using autodoc with autodoc_typehints = "description", documented varargs and kwargs (e.g. *args, **kwargs) are rendered twice and incompletely in the generated documentation. The duplication occurs during merge of signature-based type hints and docstring-parsed parameter fields.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) record_typehints() (sphinx/ext/autodoc/typehints.py) produces annotation keys using inspect.signature parameter.name (e.g. 'args', 'kwargs') — these keys do not include leading '*' characters. Location: sphinx/ext/autodoc/typehints.py:record_typehints.

2) modify_field_list() (sphinx/ext/autodoc/typehints.py) assumes parsed docstring parameter names can be used verbatim as keys and therefore stores names like '*args'/'**kwargs' into its arguments map. This mismatches the annotation keys and causes the typehint-merging logic to insert additional param/type entries. Location: sphinx/ext/autodoc/typehints.py:modify_field_list. The canonical relationship required is that parsed names must normalize to the signature key, e.g. when extracting name use name = parts[1].lstrip('*').strip() or name = ' '.join(parts[2:]).lstrip('*').strip().

3) augment_descriptions_with_types() (sphinx/ext/autodoc/typehints.py) likewise adds parsed names (including leading '*' characters) to has_description/has_type, so documented varargs/kwargs are not recognized as already described and duplicate fields are injected. Location: sphinx/ext/autodoc/typehints.py:augment_descriptions_with_types. The required normalization is the same: use name = parts[1].lstrip('*').strip(), name = ' '.join(parts[2:]).lstrip('*').strip(), or name = ' '.join(parts[1:]).lstrip('*').strip() before adding to has_description/has_type.

4) merge_typehints() (sphinx/ext/autodoc/typehints.py) coordinates merging annotations (from record_typehints) with docstring fields; because producers use non-starred names while consumers compare against possibly-starred parsed names, the mismatch leads to duplicated entries. Location: sphinx/ext/autodoc/typehints.py:merge_typehints.

5) Unverified/ancillary: the investigation did not run the test suite and did not check whether any third-party extension expects literal '*' in parsed field-name keys; that dependency, if present, could be affected by normalizing parsed names. (Investigation gap noted.)
StepsToReproduce: Create a module with a class whose __init__ has signature (self, x: int, *args: int, **kwargs: int) and docstring documenting :param x:, :param *args:, and :param **kwargs: (as in the provided example).
Enable the autodoc extension and set autodoc_typehints = "description" in conf.py.
Use autodoc to include the class in Sphinx documentation (e.g. automodule/autoclass or autodoc directives).
Build the Sphinx documentation and inspect the generated output for the class and its __init__.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Generated documentation shows a single set of parameter entries for x, *args, and **kwargs with their types and full docstring descriptions (varargs and kwargs appear once, with their types shown in the parameter list and their descriptions under the function).
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: The generated documentation shows duplicated entries: a parameter list that shows *args and **kwargs (typed) and then a second set of entries for ***args/***kwargs and ****kwargs appearing again under the __init__ details. The duplicated entries are incomplete or misplaced, so varargs/kwargs descriptions and types appear twice and some entries lack their description/type.

```

        :param **kwargs: Some integer

It will work fine if we escape `*` character like the following. But it's not officially recommended way, I believe.

problem_statement.Title: Setting rst_prolog causes top-level headings that start with a role/domain (e.g. :mod:`...`) to be dropped or not included in toctree
problem_statement.Description: When config.rst_prolog is set, documents whose top-level title is written using a role/domain inline markup (for example ":mod:`mypkg`") are not recognized as section titles and therefore do not render correctly or appear in the toctree. The problem only occurs when rst_prolog is present and the heading uses role syntax as the first heading line.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) Incorrect docinfo detection regex in sphinx/util/rst.py (module variable docinfo_re = re.compile(':\w+:.*?')). Wrong assumption: any line beginning with ":<word>:" is a docinfo metadata field. Why it is wrong: this pattern matches role/inline-role lines like ":mod:`name`" (no space after second colon) and therefore misclassifies them as docinfo. What the value represents: a heuristic to detect leading docinfo metadata lines. Why it's only correct under the current assumption: it only works if docinfo fields always occupy the form ":field: value"; it incorrectly treats other ":word:" constructs as metadata. Correct relationship/formula (as recommended by investigation): require whitespace after the second colon so only true docinfo fields match, e.g. replace with docinfo_re = re.compile("^:[A-Za-z0-9_-]+:\\s").

2) prepend_prolog in sphinx/util/rst.py assumes docinfo_re identifies only metadata. Location: sphinx/util/rst.py::prepend_prolog. Wrong assumption: that counting leading lines matching docinfo_re and inserting prolog/"<generated>" blank lines is safe for any matched lines. Why it matters: when a role line is misclassified as docinfo the function inserts a blank/prolog between the role text and the section underline (or adds a blank), breaking docutils' section title detection and causing the title to be dropped.

3) RSTParser.decorate call site applies prepend_prolog during parsing. Location: sphinx/parsers.py::RSTParser.decorate. Wrong assumption encoded here: applying prepend_prolog at this parse entry point is safe only if docinfo_re is correct. Because prepend_prolog is used here, the misclassification propagates into the actual parsing and toctree/title extraction steps.

4) Missing regression test for role-lined headings. Location: tests/test_util_rst.py (no test asserting that role syntax like ":mod:`x`" is NOT treated as docinfo). Wrong assumption: existing tests only verify conventional docinfo (":title: ...") and therefore did not catch the overly permissive regex. Evidence: existing tests pass for ":title: ..." but do not include a negative test for role syntax, allowing this regression to go unnoticed.
StepsToReproduce: sphinx-quickstart --no-sep --project mypackage --author me -v 0.1.0 --release 0.1.0 --language en docs

problem_statement.Title: autosummary: module template 'members' includes imported members despite autosummary_imported_members=False
problem_statement.Description: When generating autosummary module pages, the template variable `members` contains imported names (for example, the imported module 'os') even though conf.py sets autosummary_imported_members = False. This causes generated module autosummary blocks to list imported modules/objects.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) Unconditional population of ns['members'] from dir(obj) in sphinx/ext/autosummary/generate.py::generate_autosummary_content. Wrong assumption: ns['members'] should always equal dir(obj) regardless of the autosummary_imported_members setting. Evidence: the exact assignment ns['members'] = dir(obj) is present in generate_autosummary_content.

2) The autosummary configuration value is read and passed into the generator but not honored for module template members. Location: sphinx/ext/autosummary/__init__.py (process_generate_options -> generate_autosummary_docs(..., imported_members=app.config.autosummary_imported_members)) and sphinx/ext/autosummary/generate.py::generate_autosummary_content. Wrong assumption: because imported_members is passed into generation, generate_autosummary_content will apply it (it does not for ns['members']).

3) Inconsistent member-filtering heuristic: sphinx/ext/autosummary/generate.py::get_members already respects the imported flag using the condition
   if imported or getattr(value, '__module__', None) == obj.__name__
This is the intended membership relationship. Wrong assumption encoded in generate_autosummary_content: it does not apply the same heuristic to ns['members'] (it uses dir(obj) instead).

4) Module-object handling and dunder preservation are required for correct filtering. The investigation shows the correct relationship for deciding whether a name is imported: skip attributes when they are module objects (types.ModuleType) whose __name__ != module.__name__, and skip attributes whose getattr(value, '__module__', None) != module.__name__. Preserve dunder metadata names (names starting and ending with '__'). Wrong assumption: generate_autosummary_content assumes no special ModuleType or __module__ checks are necessary when building ns['members'].

5) ns['members'] is consumed by get_module_attrs and other routines; an incorrect ns['members'] value causes downstream mismatches between what autosummary documents and what templates render. Location: sphinx/ext/autosummary/generate.py::get_module_attrs. Wrong assumption: producing an unfiltered members list has no downstream impact.

6) Tests do not currently assert the module template ns['members'] semantics; existing tests focus on get_members. Location: tests/test_ext_autosummary.py. Wrong assumption: existing test coverage will catch this mismatch (it does not).
StepsToReproduce: Create _templates/autosummary/module.rst with a module autosummary template that iterates over members, e.g. a template that contains an autosummary block printing each item in members.
Create example.py containing: import os
Create index.rst that references example in an autosummary toctree.
In conf.py set autosummary_generate = True and autosummary_imported_members = False.
Build the documentation with Sphinx (e.g. make html or sphinx-build) and inspect generated/example.rst.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: When autosummary_imported_members is False, the template variable `members` exposed to module templates should exclude names that come from imported modules/objects (for example, 'os'). The generated module autosummary should list only the module's own attributes (and preserved dunder metadata names), not imported modules.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: The generated module autosummary includes imported names. Example generated/example.rst lists: __builtins__, __cached__, __doc__, __file__, __loader__, __name__, __package__, __spec__, os — i.e., the imported module 'os' appears in members even though autosummary_imported_members = False.

problem_statement.Title: Napoleon emits escaped trailing underscore in directive headers (visible backslash in HTML)
problem_statement.Description: When using sphinx.ext.napoleon, attribute and method names that end with a trailing underscore (for example `hello_`) are emitted into directive headers as `hello\_`, producing a visible backslash in the generated HTML.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) sphinx/ext/napoleon/docstring.py::GoogleDocstring._escape_args_and_kwargs encodes the assumption "always escape trailing underscores for all emitted RST contexts." Exact behavior: if name.endswith('_'): name = name[:-1] + '\\_' ; it also encodes leading-star escapes: if name[:2] == '**': return '\\*\\*' + name[2:] ; if name[:1] == '*': return '\\*' + name[1:]. This method produces escaped name strings that are returned to callers.

2) sphinx/ext/napoleon/docstring.py::GoogleDocstring._parse_attributes_section encodes the assumption "it is safe to insert the escaped name returned by _escape_args_and_kwargs directly into a directive header." Evidence: it emits lines.append('.. attribute:: ' + _name) where _name may already contain a literal backslash (e.g. 'hello\\_'), so the backslash is placed verbatim into the directive argument.

3) sphinx/ext/napoleon/docstring.py::GoogleDocstring._parse_methods_section encodes the same incorrect assumption for method directives: it emits lines.append('.. method:: %s' % _name) and thus will include any '\\_' present in _name verbatim in the directive header.

4) tests/test_ext_napoleon_docstring.py::(test_underscore_in_attribute) documents that some inline-field contexts expect escaped underscores in the produced RST (for example expected strings contain ':ivar arg\\_:' and ':vartype arg\\_:'), so the global escape behavior is relied upon by tests. This means removing the escape globally from _escape_args_and_kwargs would break those expected RST outputs.

5) sphinx/ext/napoleon/docstring.py::GoogleDocstring._consume_field (and related callers) propagate the escaped names returned by _escape_args_and_kwargs into the later parsing/emission steps, which is how the escaped trailing-underscore ends up in directive headers. These code locations together encode the incorrect global assumption and the places where that escaped value is used verbatim.

problem_statement.Title: HTML index shows two 'Symbols' sections/links when index entries start with mixed ASCII and non‑ASCII symbol characters
problem_statement.Description: When index entries use leading symbol characters with heterogeneous Unicode codepoints (for example '@' U+0040, '£' U+00A3, '←' U+2190), the generated HTML index contains two separate 'Symbols' sections/links instead of one. Both links appear in the index but point to the same anchor/first section.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) Wrong sentinel used for ordering in sphinx/environment/adapters/indexentries.py::IndexEntries.create_index (keyfunc). The code prefixes alphabetic/underscore keys with chr(127):
   if lckey[0:1].isalpha() or lckey.startswith('_'):
       lckey = chr(127) + lckey
   This encodes the incorrect assumption that chr(127) sorts after all symbol characters. That is false for Unicode symbols with codepoints > 127 (e.g. '£' U+00A3, '←' U+2190); as a result symbol-leading entries split into non-contiguous groups.

2) Same incorrect sentinel in sphinx/environment/adapters/indexentries.py::IndexEntries.create_index (keyfunc2) for subentry sorting. The code uses the same pattern:
   if key[0:1].isalpha() or key.startswith('_'):
       key = chr(127) + key
   Both occurrences must use the same sentinel relationship; using chr(127) here produces inconsistent ordering between top-level keys and subentries.

3) Consumer manifests duplicate headings/anchors in sphinx/builders/html/__init__.py::StandaloneHTMLBuilder.write_genindex. That method consumes create_index output and creates per-key pages/anchors and top-level links; non-contiguous symbol entries produce duplicate display keys ('Symbols') in the generated index, producing duplicate links/headings that map to the same anchor/page.

4) Tests assume ASCII-only symbol behavior: tests/test_environment_indexentries.py verifies symbol-first behavior for ASCII symbols (e.g. asserts index[0] == ('Symbols', ...)), implicitly assuming the sentinel groups all symbols. The tests do not cover non-ASCII symbol codepoints, so they did not catch the split-group behavior.
StepsToReproduce: Create Sphinx index entries that begin with the characters '@' (U+0040), '£' (U+00A3), and '←' (U+2190) in the documentation source so they appear in the general index.
Build the HTML documentation (e.g., make html).
Open the generated index page (genindex.html) and inspect the index links/headings.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: There is a single 'Symbols' section in the HTML index containing all symbol-leading entries, and a single 'Symbols' link in the index navigation that points to that section.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: The HTML index shows two 'Symbols' sections/links: one for some symbol entries (e.g. '@') placed before the letter blocks and another for other symbol entries (e.g. '£', '←') placed after the letters. Both links appear in the index but point to the same anchor/first section.

problem_statement.Title: Man page builder writes flat man pages incompatible with MANPATH
problem_statement.Description: The Sphinx man-page builder writes every generated man page directly into the build output directory as <name>.<section> (flat layout) instead of creating per-section subdirectories (man1/, man3/, ...) required by unix man/MANPATH lookup.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) sphinx/builders/manpage.py: ManualPageBuilder.write encodes the incorrect assumption that man pages belong in a flat directory. Evidence: targetname is constructed as '%s.%s' % (name, section) and the destination is computed as destination_path = path.join(self.outdir, targetname). The correct relationship for MANPATH compatibility should be: section_dir = path.join(self.outdir, 'man' + str(section)) and destination_path = path.join(section_dir, targetname).

2) tests/test_build_manpage.py encodes the same flat-layout assumption in its assertions: tests expect files at app.outdir / '<name>.<section>' instead of under app.outdir / 'man' + str(section) / '<name>.<section>', so test coverage enforces and documents the current (incorrect) layout.

3) sphinx/writers/manpage.py (ManualPageWriter/ManualPageTranslator) do not assume or control filesystem layout; they only set manual_section used in the .TH header. This identifies the builder (ManualPageBuilder.write) as the single place encoding the filesystem-layout assumption (no path logic in the writer/translator).

4) The codebase contains directory-creation utilities (sphinx/util/osutil.py: ensuredir) and existing builders that create per-output directories; the absence of such directory-creation logic in ManualPageBuilder.write is part of the encoded incorrect assumption that no per-section directories are required.
StepsToReproduce: Create or use a Sphinx project that builds man pages.
Run the man builder: sphinx-build -b man <sourcedir> <outdir> (or use `make man`).
List the build output directory: ls <outdir> and ls <outdir>/man1 (or equivalent).
Observe that generated files like <name>.<section> exist directly under <outdir> and that <outdir>/man1 (and other manN dirs) do not exist.
Optionally, attempt to use man with the build directory on MANPATH: MANPATH=<outdir> man <name> and observe man does not find the page because it expects section subdirectories.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: User-visible: Generated man pages are discoverable by the standard man/MANPATH lookup. The build output places each page in a section subdirectory (for example <outdir>/man1/<name>.1, <outdir>/man3/<name>.3, etc.), so tools that search MANPATH can find them without additional packaging steps.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: All generated man pages are written directly into the build output directory as files named <name>.<section> (for example sphinxtests.1). No per-section directories (man1/, man3/, ...) are created, so man/MANPATH cannot find the pages when pointed at the build directory.

hints_text:
I think that users should copy the generated man file to the appropriate directory. The build directory is not an appropriate directory to manage man pages. So no section directory is needed, AFAIK. I don't know why do you want to set `MANPATH` to the output directory. To check the output, you can give the path to the man file for man command like `man _build/man/sphinx-build.1`. Please let me know your purpose in detail.
From a [separate github thread](https://github.com/flux-framework/flux-core/pull/3033#issuecomment-662515605) that describes the specific use case in some more detail:
> When run in a builddir, `src/cmd/flux` sets `MANPATH` such that `man flux` will display the current builddir version of `flux.1`. This is done so that documentation matches the version of Flux being run.

Essentially, we are trying to make running in-tree look as similar to running an installed version as possible.

---

> I think that users should copy the generated man file to the appropriate directory.

On `make install`, we do have the automake setup to copy the manpages to `$prefix/man/man1`, `$prefix/man/man3`, etc.  This did require some extra work though, since each source file and its destination has to be explicitly enumerated in the automake file.  If the man pages were built into their respective sections, a recursive copy would work too.  Not a huge deal, but just another factor I wanted to bring up.
Understandable. +1 to change the structure of output directory. As commented, it causes a breaking change for users. So I propose you to add a configuration `man_make_section_directory = (True | False)` for migration. During 3.x, it defaults to False, and it will default to True on 4.0 release. What do you think?

>I’m happy to take a crack at implementing a fix, though this change in behavior may break some people who expect everything to appear in a man/ directory.

It would be very nice if you send us a PR :-)

problem_statement.Title: autodoc_typehints ignored for overloaded callables (overload signatures still show annotations)
problem_statement.Description: When autodoc_typehints is set to hide or move type annotations (for example 'none' or 'description'), overloaded callables still render their annotations in the displayed overload signatures. The configuration is honored for normal (non-overloaded) signatures but not for overload-rendering code paths.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) Overloaded-signature branches in sphinx/ext/autodoc/__init__.py assume annotation-hiding has already been applied; they call stringify_signature(overload, **kwargs) without setting show_annotation based on the autodoc_typehints config. Location: sphinx/ext/autodoc/__init__.py: FunctionDocumenter.format_signature (overload branch).

2) Same incorrect assumption in method overload rendering: MethodDocumenter.format_signature calls stringify_signature(overload, **kwargs) without applying autodoc_typehints. Location: sphinx/ext/autodoc/__init__.py: MethodDocumenter.format_signature (overload branch).

3) Same incorrect assumption for class/constructor overload rendering: ClassDocumenter.format_signature iterates overloads and calls stringify_signature(overload, **kwargs) without applying autodoc_typehints. Location: sphinx/ext/autodoc/__init__.py: ClassDocumenter.format_signature (overload/constructor branch).

4) stringify_signature has a default that causes annotations to be shown when callers omit the argument: def stringify_signature(sig: inspect.Signature, show_annotation: bool = True, show_return_annotation: bool = True) -> str. Because the overloaded-signature code paths do not set show_annotation, this default (show_annotation=True) makes annotations visible. Location: sphinx/util/inspect.py: stringify_signature.

5) The expected autodoc_typehints relationship is: if self.env.config.autodoc_typehints in ('none', 'description'): kwargs.setdefault('show_annotation', False). Non-overloaded format_args implementations already apply this check; the overloaded branches omitted it, creating the inconsistency. Location (where the correct check exists): sphinx/ext/autodoc/__init__.py: FunctionDocumenter.format_args and MethodDocumenter.format_args.

6) Investigation gap (not verified by the agent): whether hiding annotations from overload signatures under 'description' mode still results in those annotations being made available to the typehints extension (sphinx/ext/autodoc/typehints.py) so they can be inserted into parameter descriptions. Location not inspected: sphinx/ext/autodoc/typehints.py.
StepsToReproduce: In conf.py set: autodoc_typehints = 'none' (or 'description').
Create example.py with overloaded functions, e.g. using typing.overload for multiple foo signatures and a concrete implementation:
In index.rst include the module with autodoc directives, e.g.:
.. automodule:: example
:members:
:undoc-members:
Build the documentation (e.g. sphinx-build) and inspect the generated docs for the overloaded callable signatures.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: When autodoc_typehints is 'none', type annotations should not appear in the rendered documentation for the overloaded callable signatures. When autodoc_typehints is 'description', annotations should be absent from signatures and the type information should appear in the parameter/return descriptions in the documentation.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Overloaded callable signatures display type annotations in the generated documentation despite autodoc_typehints being set to 'none' or 'description'; non-overloaded signatures respect the configuration.

problem_statement.Title: autodoc_type_aliases not applied to module-level variables and class attributes
problem_statement.Description: The autodoc_type_aliases mapping in conf.py is ignored for module-level variables and class attributes, so forward-reference type names (e.g. 'String') remain unresolved in generated documentation for variables/attributes.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) Missing type_aliases when evaluating module-level annotations: DataDocumenter.add_directive_header in sphinx/ext/autodoc/__init__.py calls typing.get_type_hints(self.parent) without passing the configured mapping (self.config.autodoc_type_aliases). This encodes the incorrect assumption that get_type_hints(self.parent) without type_aliases suffices to resolve forward refs for module data.

2) Missing type_aliases when evaluating class attribute annotations: AttributeDocumenter.add_directive_header in sphinx/ext/autodoc/__init__.py calls typing.get_type_hints(self.parent) without passing self.config.autodoc_type_aliases, encoding the same incorrect assumption for class attributes.

3) Intended API/behavior demonstrated elsewhere: function/method/class documenters in the same file use the type_aliases parameter when evaluating signatures (examples: inspect.signature(..., type_aliases=self.config.autodoc_type_aliases) and evaluate_signature(..., self.config.autodoc_type_aliases)). This shows the expected relationship that evaluation routines must be given self.config.autodoc_type_aliases to resolve alias mappings.

4) String-annotation fallback is not post-processed with aliases: When get_type_hints raises NameError the code falls back to raw __annotations__ or analyzer.annotations (string form); those fallbacks are not transformed with autodoc_type_aliases. This is a separate limitation (no alias application to string-based annotations) present in the same locations.

Relationship / exact evaluation that is missing: the code currently uses
  annotations = get_type_hints(self.parent)
but to honor autodoc_type_aliases the evaluated form must include the mapping, i.e.
  annotations = get_type_hints(self.parent, None, self.config.autodoc_type_aliases)
(analogous to other documenters that pass type_aliases when evaluating signatures).
StepsToReproduce:
Create example.py with the following contents:

from __future__ import annotations

#: blah blah blah
var: String


class MyString:
    "mystring"

    #: blah blah blah
    var: String

Create index.rst containing:

.. automodule:: example
   :members:
   :undoc-members:
Build the docs with Sphinx (e.g. run sphinx-build -b html . _build/html) and inspect the generated documentation for example.var and example.MyString.var.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Documentation for module-level variables and class attributes should show their types resolved according to autodoc_type_aliases (forward-reference name 'String' should be resolved to example.MyString as specified in the mapping).
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: In the generated documentation, module variable example.var and class attribute example.MyString.var display the unresolved forward-reference name ('String') instead of the mapped type (example.MyString). Function/method signatures that already pass type_aliases are not affected.

problem_statement.Title: Sphinx 3.3: "no number is assigned for table:" warnings from mismatched env.toc_fignumbers shape in singlefile builds
problem_statement.Description: After upgrading to Sphinx 3.3, building docs with single-file/aggregated builders (singlehtml and similarly aggregated doctree paths such as latex) produces repeated "WARNING: no number is assigned for table:" messages. The warnings come from numref/figure/section-number resolution failing to find assigned numbers during cross-reference resolution.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1. StandardDomain.get_fignumber (sphinx/domains/std.py::StandardDomain.get_fignumber) assumes env.toc_fignumbers and env.toc_secnumbers are keyed by each document's docname and looks up numbers as env.toc_fignumbers[docname][figtype][id] (and env.toc_secnumbers[docname]). When a per-doc key is missing it raises ValueError and logs the warning.

2. SingleFileHTMLBuilder.assemble_toc_fignumbers (sphinx/builders/singlehtml.py::SingleFileHTMLBuilder.assemble_toc_fignumbers) produces a different mapping shape: it encodes document+figtype into alias strings and returns the mapping under the master_doc key. Concretely, it builds new_fignumbers with alias = "%s/%s" % (docname, figtype) so env.toc_fignumbers becomes { master_doc: { "%s/%s": { id: fignum } } } rather than the per-doc shape StandardDomain expects.

3. MathDomain.resolve_xref (sphinx/domains/math.py::MathDomain.resolve_xref) makes the same incorrect assumption as std: it directly indexes env.toc_fignumbers by docname and uses env.toc_fignumbers[docname]['displaymath'].get(node_id, ()) to obtain equation numbers. Because singlehtml uses the alias/master_doc shape, this lookup fails and displaymath eq numbers are treated as missing.

4. TocTreeCollector.assign_figure_numbers (sphinx/environment/collectors/toctree.py::TocTreeCollector.assign_figure_numbers) is the canonical writer that populates the per-doc mapping env.toc_fignumbers[docname][figtype][id] for normal multi-page builds; StandardDomain originally expects this canonical shape. The mismatch is therefore between this canonical shape and the singlefile alias shape.

5. Shape relationship mismatch (encoded format): consumers expect env.toc_fignumbers[docname][figtype][id], while SingleFileHTMLBuilder returns { master_doc: { "%s/%s" % (docname, figtype) : { id: fignum } } }. StandardDomain and MathDomain do not attempt the alias lookup fallback and so treat numbers as missing, producing the user-visible warning "no number is assigned for %s: %s".
StepsToReproduce: Upgrade project to Sphinx 3.3.
Build the documentation with the single-file HTML builder (singlehtml) or any build path that aggregates doctrees into a single document (e.g. the latex/aggregated doctree path reported).
Observe warnings like: "WARNING: no number is assigned for table:" in the build output and missing numbers in numref references.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Table/figure/section/displaymath references (numref/xref) are resolved and display their assigned numbers during single-file/aggregated builds; no spurious "no number is assigned for ..." warnings should appear when numbers were assigned by the collector.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Build output contains repeated warnings such as "WARNING: no number is assigned for table:" and other references (figures, displaymath) appear without assigned numbers in single-file/aggregated builds, despite numbering being assigned in the environment collector.

hints_text: I digged into this a little bit more and it seems like the `id` of the table isn't properly making it into `env.toc_fignumbers`. If I set `:name: mylabel`, regardless the I see something like this in `env.toc_fignumbers` ``` 'pagename': {'table': {'id3': (1,)}, ``` So it seems like `id3` is being used for the table id instead of `mylabel` @choldgraf I suspect it's related to this: https://github.com/sphinx-doc/sphinx/commit/66dda1fc50249e9da62e79380251d8795b8e36df. Oooohhh good find! 👏👏👏 Confirmed that this was the issue - we had been referencing Tables that didn't have a title with `numref`, and this bugfix (I guess it was a bugfix?) caused us to start raising errors. Perhaps this restriction about tables needing a title could be documented more clearly? The `numfig` option has been described as follows. >If true, figures, tables and code-blocks are automatically numbered if they have a caption. https://www.sphinx-doc.org/en/master/usage/configuration.html#confval-numfig It says a table not having a title is not assigned a number. Then `numfig` can't refer it because of no table number. > It says a table not having a title is not assigned a number. Then `numfig` can't refer it because of no table number. This means that a user is not able to add a numbered table with no caption correct? I could understand such restrictions for Jupyter Book but it doesn't make a lot of sense for Sphinx IMO. I think Sphinx should allow users to have enumerable nodes with no caption. What do you think @choldgraf? >This means that a user is not able to add a numbered table with no caption correct? Yes. Since the beginning, numfig feature only supports captioned figures and tables. I don't know how many people want to assign numbers to non-captioned items. But this is the first feature request, AFAIK. I think my take is that I don't think it is super useful to be able to have numbered references for things that don't have titles/captions. However, it also didn't feel like it *shouldn't* be possible, and so I assumed that it was possible (and thus ran into what I thought was a bug). I think it would be more helpful to surface a more informative warning like "You attempted to add a numbered reference to a Table without a title, add a title for this to work." (or, surface this gotcha in the documentation more obviously like with a `warning` or `note` directive?) @tk0miya @choldgraf both make good points for restricting `figure` and `table` directives with no caption. My issue is that this is done at the enumerable node which implies that **all** enumerable nodes with no title/caption are skipped - not just `figure` and `table`. > Since the beginning, numfig feature only supports captioned figures and tables. Just to clarify, `numfig` feature has - prior to v3.3.0 - supported uncaptioned tables but it did not display the caption. The user was able to reference the table using `numref` role (see example below). In the event that the user tried to reference the caption (aka `name` placeholder), Sphinx threw a warning indicating that there was no caption. This solution seemed sensible to me because it allowed other extensions to utilize enumerable nodes regardless of caption/no caption restriction. My main motivation for wanting to revert back or restrict the bugfix to tables and figures is because both the extensions I've worked on depend on the utilization of enumerable nodes regardless of captions/no captions. I think it wouldn't be too difficult to add the information to `env.toc_fignumbers` but I wanted to make a case before I addressed this in [sphinx-proof](https://github.com/executablebooks/sphinx-proof) and [sphinx-exercise](https://github.com/executablebooks/sphinx-exercise). **Example** Sphinx Version - v3.2.1 ````md ```{list-table} :header-rows: 1 :name: table1 * - Training - Validation * - 0 - 5 * - 13720 - 2744 ``` Referencing table using `numref`: {numref}`table1`. ```{list-table} Caption here :header-rows: 1 :name: table2 * - Training - Validation * - 0 - 5 * - 13720 - 2744 ``` Referencing table using `numref`: {numref}`table2`. ```` So it sounds like the `tl;dr` from @najuzilu is that in other extensions, she is *using* the fact that you can reference non-captioned elements with a number, and that Sphinx now removing this ability is breaking those extensions. Is that right? That's correct @choldgraf This is a screenshot of the PDF that is generated from @najuzilu 's example with v3.2.1. As you see, it does not work correctly in LaTeX output. I'd not like to support assigning numbers to no captioned items until fixed this (if somebody needs it).

problem_statement.Title: Sphinx 3.2 rejects option:: tokens containing '[' or '=' (e.g. "[enable=]PATTERN")
problem_statement.Description: When building docs with Sphinx 3.2, option:: directive tokens that contain characters like '[' or '=' (for example "[enable=]PATTERN") are treated as malformed; Sphinx emits a "Malformed option description" warning and the token is skipped/unindexed. Earlier Sphinx versions accepted such tokens.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) option_desc_re encodes a restrictive assumption about valid option syntax (location: sphinx/domains/std.py). The regex is defined as re.compile(r'((?:/|--|-|\+)?[^\s=[]+)(=?\s*.*)') which forbids '[' and '=' in the captured option-name group, so tokens containing '[' or '=' (e.g. "[enable=]PATTERN") do not match.

2) Cmdoption.handle_signature treats any non-match of option_desc_re as an invalid option (location: sphinx/domains/std.py, class Cmdoption, method handle_signature). The code checks m = option_desc_re.match(potential_option); if not m it logs a warning "Malformed option description ..." and continues, thereby skipping the token. This encodes the assumption that a non-matching token must be malformed and must not be indexed or rendered.

3) The warning message string itself encodes the restrictive expectation of allowed forms (location: sphinx/domains/std.py, same method). The hardcoded message lists only a few forms ("opt", "-opt args", "--opt args", "/opt args", "+opt args"), making the restrictive assumption visible to users and masking valid other syntaxes.

4) Downstream indexing and cross-reference resolution depend on the canonical names produced by handle_signature (locations: Cmdoption.add_target_and_index, StandardDomain.add_program_option, StandardDomain._resolve_option_xref in sphinx/domains/std.py). Because handle_signature skips non-matching tokens, those options are not stored in StandardDomain.progoptions and therefore are not indexed or resolvable.

5) Concrete consequence for the reported case: a token containing '[' and '=' fails option_desc_re.match, triggers the warning-and-skip branch in handle_signature, and so the option is omitted from the generated output/index; when warnings are treated as errors this breaks the build (location chain: sphinx/domains/std.py as above).
StepsToReproduce:
Create an rst file containing an option directive using a token with '[' and '=': for example:

.. option:: [enable=]PATTERN

   Immediately enable events matching PATTERN
Run Sphinx 3.2 to build the documentation (for example: sphinx-build -b html sourcedir builddir).
Observe the build log: a warning like "Malformed option description '[enable=]PATTERN', should look like ..." appears (and may be treated as an error). The option is not indexed/rendered as expected.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Sphinx accepts program option syntax strings that are valid for the documented program (including tokens containing '[' or '='), does not emit a "Malformed option description" warning for such tokens, and renders/indexes the option so it is visible and resolvable in the generated documentation.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Sphinx 3.2 logs a "Malformed option description '[enable=]PATTERN' ..." warning for the option:: token and skips it; the option is not indexed or rendered, and the build can fail if warnings are treated as errors.

hints_text:
I disagree with 

> Sphinx ideally shouldn't change in ways that break the building of documentation that worked in older versions, because this makes it unworkably difficult to have documentation that builds with whatever the Linux distro's sphinx-build is.

The idea that things shouldn't change to avoid breaking is incredibly toxic developer culture. This is what pinned versions are for, additionally, you can have your project specify a minimum and maximum sphinx as a requirement.
I agree that there's some philosophical differences at play here. Our project wants to be able to build on a fairly wide range of supported and shipping distributions (we go for "the versions of major distros still supported by the distro vendor", roughly), and we follow the usual/traditional C project/Linux distro approach of "build with the versions of libraries, dependencies and tools shipped by the build platform" generally. At the moment that means we need our docs to build with Sphinx versions ranging from 1.6 through to 3.2, and the concept of a "pinned version" just doesn't exist in this ecosystem. Being able to build with the distro version of Sphinx is made much more awkward if the documentation markup language is not a well specified and stable target for documentation authors to aim at.

Incidentally, the current documentation of the option:: directive in https://www.sphinx-doc.org/en/master/usage/restructuredtext/domains.html?highlight=option#directive-option says nothing about this requirement for -, --, / or +.

For the moment I've dealt with this by rewriting the fragment of documentation to avoid the option directive. I don't want to get into an argument if the Sphinx project doesn't feel that strong backward-compatibility guarantees are a project goal, so I thought I'd just write up my suggestions/hopes for sphinx-build more generally for you to consider (or reject!) and leave it at that:

* Where directives/markup have a required syntax for their arguments, it would be useful if the documentation clearly and precisely described the syntax. That allows documentation authors to know whether they're using something as intended.
* Where possible, the initial implementation should start with tightly parsing that syntax and diagnosing errors. It's much easier to loosen restrictions or use a previously forbidden syntax for a new purpose if older implementations just rejected it rather than if they accepted it and did something different because they didn't parse it very strictly.
* Where major changes are necessary, a reasonable length period of deprecation and parallel availability of old and new syntax helps to ease transitions.

and on a more general note I would appreciate it if the project considered the needs of external non-Python projects that have adopted Sphinx as a documentation system but which don't necessarily have the same control over tooling versions that Python-ecosystem projects might. (The Linux kernel is another good example here.)

> Where major changes are necessary, a reasonable length period of deprecation and parallel availability of old and new syntax helps to ease transitions.

Major versions are done via semver, where Sphinx 2 is a major breaking change over Sphinx 1, and Sphinx 3 breaks changes over Sphinx 2. What other things could be done? The concept of deprecation isn't as common in Python communities due to the popularity of fixed versions or locking to a major version. IE ``pip install sphinx==3`` which installs the latest major sphinx version of 3.
This change was added at https://github.com/sphinx-doc/sphinx/pull/7770. It is not an expected change. It means this is a mere bug.

problem_statement.Title: autodoc treats explicit empty __all__ ([]) as if __all__ is absent
problem_statement.Description: When a module defines __all__ = [] (explicitly exporting nothing), autodoc's automodule :members: still documents all module members. The module-level explicit empty __all__ is being ignored.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) sphinx/ext/autodoc/__init__.py::ModuleDocumenter.import_object assigns self.__all__ = inspect.getall(self.object). sphinx.util.inspect.getall(obj) returns None when the module has no __all__ and returns a Sequence (possibly empty, e.g. []) when the module defines __all__. The correct semantic relationship: self.__all__ is None <=> module has no __all__; self.__all__ == [] <=> module explicitly exports nothing.

2) sphinx/ext/autodoc/__init__.py::ModuleDocumenter.get_object_members uses a truthiness check ("if not self.__all__:") to detect the absence of __all__. This conflates None (absent) and [] (explicit empty), so an explicit empty __all__ is treated as if there is no __all__, causing autodoc to document all members instead of none.

3) sphinx/ext/autodoc/__init__.py::ModuleDocumenter.sort_members uses a truthiness check ("if order == 'bysource' and self.__all__:") to decide whether to apply __all__-based ordering. This also conflates absence and explicit empty and causes incorrect ordering/selection behavior when __all__ == [].

4) sphinx/util/inspect.py::getall encodes the intended contract (returns None for missing __all__, returns a Sequence for present __all__). Autodoc code relies on that contract and must distinguish presence (is None / is not None) from truthiness.

5) sphinx/ext/autodoc/mock.py (mock modules) sets __all__ = [] to represent modules that explicitly export nothing; treating [] as absent would break mocks/tests. All findings are equal-weight evidence that autodoc uses truthiness where it must check presence (None vs Sequence).
StepsToReproduce: Create example.py with contents: __all__ = [] and several functions (e.g. foo, bar, baz) each with docstrings.
Create an index.rst that uses .. automodule:: example with the :members: option.
Build the Sphinx documentation (e.g. run sphinx-build on the project).
Observe the automodule output for example.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Because the module explicitly sets __all__ = [], the automodule :members: listing should show no members (no entries for foo/bar/baz).
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: All module members (foo, bar, baz) are shown under automodule :members: despite __all__ being an explicit empty list.

problem_statement.Title: autodoc fails to resolve struct.Struct type annotations (emits unqualified 'Struct')
problem_statement.Description: When a type annotation uses struct.Struct, Sphinx/autodoc emits an unqualified name "Struct" and fails to resolve it (error: "class reference target not found: Struct"). The problem appears during annotation stringification and affects cross-reference resolution.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) Incorrect assumption in sphinx/util/typing.py::stringify: the code treats getattr(annotation, '__module__', None) == 'builtins' as sufficient evidence that the object is a real builtin and returns annotation.__qualname__ (unqualified name). Current behavior: if __module__ == 'builtins' then stringify -> annotation.__qualname__ (e.g. "Struct"). Required relationship (from investigation): omit the module prefix only when (annotation.__module__ in ('__builtin__','builtins')) AND the object is actually present in builtins.__dict__.values().

2) Incorrect assumption in sphinx/util/typing.py::restify: restify checks cls.__module__ in ('__builtin__','builtins') and omits the module name (emits ':class:`%s`' % cls.__name__) based solely on the module-name string. This duplicates the brittle logic in stringify and misclassifies extension types (e.g. struct.Struct).

3) Incorrect assumption in sphinx/ext/inheritance_diagram.py::class_name: class_name uses module in ('__builtin__','builtins') to decide to drop the module prefix and produce cls.__name__. That same module-string-only test causes mislabeling of types whose __module__ equals 'builtins' but which are not actually defined in the builtins module object.

4) Dependent expectation in sphinx/domains/python.py::_parse_annotation and type_to_xref: the python domain expects annotation text to be fully-qualified when necessary and uses that text as pending_xref.reftarget. When stringify/restify produce unqualified names (e.g. "Struct"), the domain cannot resolve the reftarget to the actual object (e.g. 'struct.Struct'), producing "class reference target not found: Struct". (This is an observed consequence of the above incorrect assumptions.)
StepsToReproduce: Create docs/index.rst containing an automodule directive for your module (example shows helloworld.helloworld).

Create a module file (helloworld.py) with the following contents: import struct; import pathlib; def consume_struct(_: struct.Struct) -> None: pass; def make_struct() -> struct.Struct: mystruct = struct.Struct('HH'); return mystruct; def make_path() -> pathlib.Path: return pathlib.Path()
Run Sphinx build: python3 -m sphinx -b html docs/ doc-out -nvWT
Observe build warnings/errors about "class reference target not found: Struct" and that the struct.Struct annotations are not resolved/linked.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Annotations using struct.Struct are displayed as fully-qualified (e.g. 'struct.Struct') and resolved/linked by autodoc in the generated documentation, analogous to how pathlib.Path is resolved.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Sphinx/autodoc emits the unqualified name "Struct" for struct.Struct annotations and fails to resolve it (build warning/error: "class reference target not found: Struct"). struct.Struct does not appear as a resolved/linked type in the output.

hints_text:
Unfortunately, the `struct.Struct` class does not have the correct module-info. So it is difficult to support.
```
Python 3.8.2 (default, Mar  2 2020, 00:44:41)
[Clang 11.0.0 (clang-1100.0.33.17)] on darwin
Type "help", "copyright", "credits" or "license" for more information.
>>> import struct
>>> struct.Struct.__module__
'builtins'
```

Note: In python3.9, it returns the correct module-info. But it answers the internal module name: `_struct`.
```
Python 3.9.1 (default, Dec 18 2020, 00:18:40)
[Clang 11.0.3 (clang-1103.0.32.59)] on darwin
Type "help", "copyright", "credits" or "license" for more information.
>>> import struct
>>> struct.Struct.__module__
'_struct'
```

So it would better to use `autodoc_type_aliases` to correct it forcedly.
```
# helloworld.py
from __future__ import annotations  # important!
from struct import Struct

def consume_struct(_: Struct) -> None:
    pass
```
```
# conf.py
autodoc_type_aliases = {
    'Struct': 'struct.Struct',
}
```

Then, it working fine.

problem_statement.Title: napoleon_use_param ignored for "Other parameters" section
problem_statement.Description: The "Other parameters" section is always formatted with the grouped/list style produced by _format_fields, ignoring the napoleon_use_param setting. This causes inconsistent rendering between the "Parameters" and "Other parameters" sections.
StepsToReproduce: Open a Python REPL in an environment with Sphinx and napoleon available.
Run: from sphinx.ext.napoleon import NumpyDocstring

Run: print(str(NumpyDocstring("""
Parameters
----------
x : int

Other parameters
----------------
y: float
""")))
Observe the printed docstring rendering: the Parameters section uses :param/:type lines while the Other parameters section is rendered as a grouped header (e.g. ':Other Parameters: **y** (*float*)').
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: When napoleon_use_param is True, the "Other parameters" section should be formatted like the "Parameters" section in user-visible output — i.e., emit docutils param roles such as ':param <name>:' and ':type <name>: <type>' for entries listed under "Other parameters".
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: The "Other parameters" section is rendered with a grouped/list style header (e.g. ':Other Parameters: **y** (*float*)') regardless of napoleon_use_param, while the "Parameters" section follows napoleon_use_param and emits ':param' and ':type' lines.

problem_statement.Title: viewcode generates module pages for epub builds even when viewcode_enable_epub=False
problem_statement.Description: When building HTML and EPUB together (make html epub) the sphinx.ext.viewcode extension creates module pages for the EPUB output even though conf.py sets viewcode_enable_epub = False.
problem_statement.RootCause: 1) sphinx/ext/viewcode.py::collect_pages encodes the incorrect assumption that page-generation is safe for any builder that emits the 'html-collect-pages' event; collect_pages is registered via app.connect('html-collect-pages', collect_pages) but does not check app.builder.name or env.config.viewcode_enable_epub before yielding pages. 2) sphinx/ext/viewcode.py::doctree_read encodes the intended policy by checking the epub/config guard: if app.builder.name.startswith("epub") and not env.config.viewcode_enable_epub: return — collect_pages does not mirror this guard, causing an inconsistency. 3) sphinx/builders/html/__init__.py::gen_pages_from_extensions emits the 'html-collect-pages' event for all StandaloneHTMLBuilder subclasses (including EpubBuilder), so any handler that does not perform a per-builder/config check will run during epub builds. 4) sphinx/builders/_epub_base.py::EpubBuilder inherits gen_pages_from_extensions and sets out_suffix = '.xhtml', so EpubBuilder triggers collect_pages; viewcode code uses builder.out_suffix (via should_generate_module_page / get_module_filename) to compute filenames for generated pages. 5) Constants referenced by viewcode (OUTPUT_DIRNAME = '_modules', StandaloneHTMLBuilder.out_suffix = '.html', EpubBuilder.out_suffix = '.xhtml') are not incorrect and do not cause this behavior; they remain valid. 6) Investigation/testing gap: no multi-builder run was executed to verify env._viewcode_modules lifecycle across sequential builds (html then epub in a shared environment), so potential state/persistence edge cases were not exercised. 7) The explicit guard that must be present in collect_pages (to match doctree_read) is: if app.builder.name.startswith("epub") and not env.config.viewcode_enable_epub: return
StepsToReproduce: In conf.py set viewcode_enable_epub = False
Run: make html epub
Inspect the EPUB build output (and build staging directories) for generated module pages (e.g. the _modules output and generated .xhtml module pages)
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: When viewcode_enable_epub is False, the viewcode extension does not create module pages for EPUB builds; no module pages or _modules output for the epub builder should appear in the EPUB output.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Module pages (the _modules output and generated module pages) are produced for the EPUB build even though viewcode_enable_epub is set to False.

problem_statement.Title: autodoc: annotation-only inherited class attribute treated as undocumented
problem_statement.Description: When documenting a subclass with ``.. autoclass:: Bar`` plus ``:members:`` and ``:inherited-members:``, an annotation-only attribute declared in the superclass with an inline ``#:`` comment (PEP-526 style) is omitted (treated as undocumented). The attribute only appears if ``:undoc-members:`` is explicitly given.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) sphinx/ext/autodoc/importer.py::get_class_members encodes the incorrect assumption "if a member name already exists in members, do not attach annotation/analyzer metadata" (expressed by `if name not in members` gates). This prevents analyzer-provided docstrings and class ownership from being merged into pre-existing ObjectMember entries.

2) sphinx/ext/autodoc/__init__.py::Documenter.filter_members assumes ObjectMember instances will carry analyzer docstrings (it prefers `obj.docstring` before checking analyzer.attr_docs). Because importer.get_class_members did not merge analyzer data into existing ObjectMember.docstring, filter_members treats the attribute as undocumented and filters it out.

3) sphinx/pycode/__init__.py::ModuleAnalyzer.attr_docs correctly collects inline attribute comments keyed by (qualname, name). The analyzer data exists for the annotation-only attribute; the bug is that importer.get_class_members fails to attach/merge that data into members entries when a placeholder already exists.

4) The correct relationship that must hold (and is currently violated) is: when processing annotation-only attributes and analyzer.attr_docs in get_class_members, either create members[name] = ObjectMember(name, INSTANCEATTR, class_=cls[, docstring]) if absent, or, if members[name] already exists and is an ObjectMember, merge the analyzer metadata into it: set existing.docstring = analyzer_docstring (if any) and if existing.class_ is None then set existing.class_ = cls; only set existing.object to INSTANCEATTR when no runtime object exists. The current code's "skip when name exists" logic prevents enforcing this relationship.

5) The malfunctioning gate in importer.get_class_members is the single root cause that explains the observed omission; sibling code paths for module-level members show the intended pattern of attaching analyzer.attr_docs to ObjectMember entries during creation rather than skipping merge.
StepsToReproduce:
Create example.py containing:

class Foo:
    """docstring"""
    attr1: int  #: docstring

class Bar(Foo):
    """docstring"""
    attr2: str  #: docstring

Create index.rst containing:

.. autoclass:: example.Bar
   :members:
   :inherited-members:
Run Sphinx (e.g. sphinx-build -b html . _build) and inspect the generated documentation for example.Bar
Observe that attr2 is documented but attr1 (inherited, annotation-only with inline comment) is not shown unless :undoc-members: is added to the autoclass directive
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: The inherited annotation-only attribute (Foo.attr1 with inline "#: docstring") should appear in the generated documentation for Bar when using ``:members:`` and ``:inherited-members:`` (without requiring ``:undoc-members:``). The inline attribute comment should be shown as the attribute's documentation.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: The inherited annotation-only attribute is omitted from the subclass documentation (treated as undocumented). The attribute only appears if ``:undoc-members:`` is additionally specified.
